title: Negotiation Fundamentals in Engineering Projects
description: Learn the fundamental principles of negotiation and how engineers, project managers, clients, contractors and suppliers can prepare for, conduct and conclude negotiations in engineering projects.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Explain what negotiation is and why it is important in engineering projects.
Identify common negotiation situations throughout the project lifecycle.
Distinguish between interests, positions, needs and demands.
Understand the difference between distributive and integrative negotiation.
Explain the importance of preparation, objectives, priorities and negotiation authority.
Recognize the role of alternatives, reservation points and bargaining ranges.
Apply effective questioning, listening and communication techniques during negotiations.
Understand how trust, relationships, power and information influence negotiation outcomes.
Recognize common negotiation mistakes and unethical practices.
Describe how negotiated outcomes should be documented and implemented.

sections.title: What Is Negotiation?
content: Negotiation is a structured process in which two or more parties communicate to reach an agreement on matters where their interests are not fully aligned.
The parties may agree on the overall objective but disagree about price, scope, responsibility, risk, time, quality or contractual terms.
Negotiation is not limited to formal contract discussions. It also occurs during planning, design coordination, procurement, project execution, change management and dispute resolution.
The purpose of negotiation is not necessarily to defeat the other party. The objective is to reach an outcome that is acceptable and implementable for the parties involved.

sections.title: Example: Negotiation in an Engineering Project
content: A shipowner wants a ballast water treatment system installed during a scheduled dry-docking period.
The supplier can meet the technical requirements but needs two additional installation days.
The shipowner wants to avoid extending the dry dock, while the supplier wants sufficient time to complete the work safely.
The parties negotiate the work sequence, resources, testing plan, responsibilities and possible additional cost.

sections.title: Why Engineers Need Negotiation Skills
content: Engineers regularly make decisions that affect cost, schedule, risk and contractual responsibility.
Technical discussions often become negotiations because the parties have different priorities or interpretations.
An engineer may need to negotiate technical requirements, design changes, testing criteria, delivery dates, resource allocation or responsibility for additional work.
Strong technical knowledge is important, but it does not automatically produce a good negotiated outcome.
Engineers must also understand communication, commercial consequences, contractual limits and stakeholder interests.

sections.title: Where Negotiation Occurs in the Project Lifecycle
content: During project initiation, stakeholders may negotiate objectives, priorities and funding.
During planning and design, project participants may negotiate scope boundaries, technical solutions and responsibilities.
During procurement, clients and suppliers negotiate price, delivery, performance requirements, warranties and risk allocation.
During execution, negotiations may concern delays, changes, interfaces, payment and access to resources.
During commissioning and handover, the parties may negotiate acceptance criteria, defects, documentation and outstanding work.
Negotiation is therefore a continuous project activity rather than a single event.

sections.title: Negotiation Is Different from Discussion
content: A discussion is an exchange of information, opinions or ideas.
A negotiation involves issues on which the parties seek an agreement.
A technical discussion may develop into a negotiation when a proposed solution affects cost, time, responsibility or contractual obligations.
Project participants should recognize when a conversation has become a negotiation because authority, preparation and documentation may then be required.

sections.title: From Technical Discussion to Negotiation
content: A supplier suggests using an alternative valve because the specified model is unavailable.
The parties first discuss whether the alternative is technically acceptable.
The conversation becomes a negotiation when they begin discussing price reduction, approval responsibility, delivery risk and warranty consequences.

sections.title: Parties in a Negotiation
content: A negotiation may involve individuals, departments or separate organizations.
Typical parties include owners, clients, contractors, consultants, suppliers, operators and regulatory representatives.
Each party may include several participants with different roles.
A negotiation team may contain technical, commercial, contractual and managerial personnel.
The parties should understand who represents whom and who has authority to make commitments.

sections.title: Negotiation Authority
content: Negotiation authority is the formal right to make offers, accept conditions or commit an organization.
A person may participate in a negotiation without having authority to approve the final agreement.
Authority limits may relate to price, risk, scope, schedule or legal terms.
Before negotiations begin, participants should understand their own authority and the likely authority of the other party.
An agreement made by someone without proper authority may require later approval or may not be binding.

sections.title: Do Not Assume Authority
content: A senior job title does not always mean that a person may accept all commercial or contractual terms.
Before relying on a commitment, confirm whether the representative is authorized to make it.
Important agreements should be approved and documented through the correct organizational process.

sections.title: Positions
content: A position is the specific outcome a party says it wants.
Positions are often expressed as demands, proposals or conditions.
Examples include a required price, a delivery date, a contractual clause or a requested technical solution.
Positions are visible, but they do not always reveal why the party wants that outcome.
Negotiations that focus only on positions can become rigid and confrontational.

sections.title: Interests
content: An interest is the underlying reason, concern or objective behind a stated position.
Interests may relate to cost, operational reliability, safety, reputation, cash flow, resource availability or risk exposure.
Different positions may sometimes satisfy the same underlying interest.
Understanding interests creates more opportunities for mutually acceptable solutions.
Effective negotiators ask why a condition matters rather than reacting only to the stated demand.

sections.title: Position versus Interest
content: A contractor demands an additional two weeks to complete the work. This is the contractor's position.
The underlying interest may be the need to avoid unsafe overtime, limited specialist availability or uncertainty about access to the worksite.
The client may be able to address the interest through additional shifts, improved access or revised work sequencing without granting the full extension.

sections.title: Needs, Wants and Preferences
content: A need is an essential requirement that must be satisfied.
A want is an outcome a party would like to obtain but may be willing to trade.
A preference is one acceptable option among several possibilities.
Negotiation preparation should distinguish between these categories.
Treating every preference as a non-negotiable need makes agreement more difficult.

sections.title: Distributive Negotiation
content: Distributive negotiation concerns how a limited amount of value will be divided between the parties.
This is sometimes described as fixed-pie negotiation.
Price negotiations are often partly distributive because a higher price benefits the seller and increases the buyer's cost.
Distributive negotiation commonly involves opening offers, concessions, reservation points and bargaining pressure.
A purely distributive approach may be appropriate for some single-issue transactions but can damage long-term relationships if used aggressively.

sections.title: Example: Distributive Negotiation
content: A supplier offers an engineering package for EUR 250,000.
The client's maximum budget is EUR 220,000.
The supplier is unwilling to accept less than EUR 210,000.
The bargaining range lies between EUR 210,000 and EUR 220,000 if no other terms are changed.

sections.title: Integrative Negotiation
content: Integrative negotiation seeks to create value before deciding how that value should be distributed.
The parties explore differences in priorities, resources, risk tolerance and timing.
A solution may improve the outcome for both parties without requiring one party to surrender an equally valuable benefit.
Integrative negotiation is particularly useful when several issues are negotiable.
It requires information sharing, problem-solving and a willingness to understand underlying interests.

sections.title: Example: Creating Value
content: A supplier cannot reduce the equipment price significantly.
However, the supplier can include operator training, extend the warranty and provide spare parts at relatively low internal cost.
These elements may be highly valuable to the client.
The parties create a more attractive total agreement without focusing only on the equipment price.

sections.title: Mixed-Motive Negotiation
content: Most engineering negotiations contain both cooperative and competitive elements.
The parties may benefit from solving a problem together while still competing over price, liability or risk.
This is known as a mixed-motive negotiation.
Effective negotiators cooperate to create value while protecting their own essential interests.
Excessive competition may prevent value creation, while excessive cooperation may lead to unnecessary concessions.

sections.title: Negotiation Preparation
content: Preparation is one of the strongest predictors of negotiation performance.
A negotiator should understand the issues, objectives, priorities, alternatives, authority limits and possible interests of the other party.
Preparation should include technical, commercial, contractual and relational considerations.
The team should also decide who will lead, who will provide specialist input and who will record agreements.
Poor preparation creates a risk of accepting unfavourable conditions or making commitments without understanding their consequences.

sections.title: Defining Negotiation Objectives
content: Objectives describe what the negotiator wants to achieve.
A target objective is the preferred realistic outcome.
A minimum acceptable outcome defines the point below which an agreement should not normally be accepted.
A negotiator may also define an ambitious opening position.
Objectives should be prioritized because not every issue has equal importance.
Clear objectives help the negotiator evaluate proposals during the discussion.

sections.title: Example: Negotiation Objectives
content: A client purchasing a new pump package may define the following objectives:
Target price: EUR 180,000.
Maximum acceptable price: EUR 195,000.
Required delivery: no later than 1 September.
Preferred warranty: 24 months.
Minimum acceptable warranty: 18 months.
Operator training is desirable but may be exchanged for a lower price.

sections.title: Priorities and Tradeable Issues
content: Negotiators should identify which issues are essential and which can be exchanged.
Issues may include price, payment timing, delivery date, technical scope, warranty, testing, documentation, training and liability.
The parties often value these issues differently.
A term that has low cost to one party may have high value to the other.
These differences create opportunities for trades that improve the total agreement.

sections.title: Negotiation Alternatives
content: An alternative is the course of action available if no agreement is reached.
A strong alternative reduces dependence on the current negotiation.
A weak alternative may increase pressure to reach an agreement.
Negotiators should identify realistic alternatives before deciding whether a proposal is acceptable.
An agreement should generally be preferred only if it is better than the best available alternative.

sections.title: Example: Alternative to an Agreement
content: A client is negotiating with Supplier A.
Supplier B can provide similar equipment at a slightly higher price but with a longer delivery time.
The offer from Supplier B is an alternative if the client cannot reach an acceptable agreement with Supplier A.
The client should compare the complete consequences of both options rather than considering price alone.

sections.title: Reservation Point
content: The reservation point is the least favourable outcome a party is willing to accept before choosing its alternative.
For a buyer, this may be the maximum acceptable total cost.
For a seller, it may be the minimum acceptable price or commercial return.
A reservation point should be based on analysis rather than emotion.
Negotiators should protect this information because revealing it can weaken their bargaining position.

sections.title: Bargaining Range
content: A bargaining range exists when the parties' acceptable outcomes overlap.
For example, if a buyer can pay up to EUR 200,000 and a seller can accept EUR 185,000, a possible bargaining range exists between those amounts.
If the acceptable outcomes do not overlap, agreement may require changes to other terms or the creation of additional value.
The parties may not know each other's true limits.
Negotiation is partly a process of discovering whether an acceptable agreement zone exists.

sections.title: Information in Negotiation
content: Information influences negotiation power and decision quality.
Relevant information may include market prices, supplier capacity, project deadlines, technical alternatives, cost drivers and legal obligations.
A negotiator should determine what information to share, what to request and what to protect.
Sharing selected information about interests may support joint problem-solving.
However, disclosing confidential limits or weak alternatives may reduce bargaining strength.

sections.title: Information Should Be Shared Deliberately
content: Complete secrecy can prevent collaborative solutions.
Complete openness can expose the organization unnecessarily.
Effective negotiators share information that supports problem-solving while protecting sensitive commercial and strategic information.

sections.title: Power in Negotiation
content: Negotiation power is the ability to influence the process or outcome.
Power may come from strong alternatives, specialist knowledge, time flexibility, market position, contractual rights or control of essential resources.
Power is not determined only by company size.
A small supplier may have significant power if it owns unique technology or is the only available source.
Power can change during the project as deadlines approach or alternatives disappear.

sections.title: Example: Changing Negotiation Power
content: Early in procurement, a client may have several qualified suppliers and strong bargaining power.
After selecting one supplier and designing the system around that supplier's equipment, switching becomes difficult.
The supplier's bargaining power may therefore increase during execution.

sections.title: Time Pressure
content: Deadlines can influence negotiation behaviour and bargaining power.
A party with an urgent need may accept less favourable conditions.
Artificial deadlines may also be used as a negotiation tactic.
Negotiators should determine whether a deadline is real, flexible or strategically presented.
Time pressure should not prevent proper technical, commercial or contractual evaluation.

sections.title: Opening the Negotiation
content: The opening phase establishes the tone, purpose and practical structure of the negotiation.
The parties may confirm participants, authority, agenda, time available and issues to be discussed.
A constructive opening helps create professional dialogue.
The negotiator should clarify the problem and avoid moving immediately into concessions before understanding the situation.
For complex negotiations, the parties may first agree on process before negotiating substance.

sections.title: Opening Offers
content: An opening offer is the first concrete proposal presented in a negotiation.
Opening offers can influence expectations and the range of later discussion.
An ambitious but credible opening may provide room for movement.
An unrealistic offer may damage trust or cause the other party to disengage.
Opening positions should be supported by objective reasoning where possible.

sections.title: Anchoring
content: Anchoring occurs when an initial number or proposal influences later judgments.
The first price mentioned may become a reference point even when it is not objectively justified.
Negotiators should prepare independent estimates before being exposed to the other party's anchor.
An unreasonable anchor can be challenged using market data, cost analysis or alternative proposals.
Responding too quickly to an anchor may unintentionally give it legitimacy.

sections.title: Example: Responding to an Anchor
content: A contractor requests EUR 500,000 for additional work.
The client should not immediately negotiate downward from that number.
The client should first request the cost breakdown, contractual basis and supporting documentation.
An independent assessment may show that the reasonable value is substantially lower.

sections.title: Asking Effective Questions
content: Questions help reveal interests, assumptions, constraints and priorities.
Open questions encourage explanation.
Clarifying questions reduce ambiguity.
Testing questions explore how the other party might respond to alternatives.
Questions should be used to understand rather than to trap or embarrass the other party.
A well-timed question can be more effective than a strong argument.

sections.title: Useful Negotiation Questions
content: What is the main reason this delivery date is important to you?
Which technical requirements create the largest cost impact?
What would need to change for you to accept this proposal?
How would this option affect your production schedule?
Which parts of the agreement are most important to your organization?

sections.title: Active Listening in Negotiation
content: Active listening helps negotiators identify information that is not immediately visible.
The listener should pay attention to facts, concerns, priorities and emotional signals.
Summarizing the other party's position can confirm understanding and reduce defensiveness.
Listening does not mean agreeing.
It means understanding the other party accurately before developing a response.

sections.title: Example: Listening Without Agreeing
content: A contractor says that a delay was caused entirely by late client information.
The project manager responds: 'I understand that you believe the drawing delay affected your work sequence. Let us review the dates and identify which activities were actually affected.'
The project manager acknowledges the concern without accepting liability before the evidence is reviewed.

sections.title: Proposals
content: A proposal presents a possible solution or set of terms.
Effective proposals are clear, specific and conditional where appropriate.
Instead of making isolated concessions, negotiators can link issues together.
For example: 'We can accept the revised delivery date if you include additional commissioning support at no extra cost.'
Conditional proposals protect value and encourage reciprocal movement.

sections.title: Concessions
content: A concession is a movement from a previous position.
Concessions may concern price, time, scope, warranty, risk or other conditions.
Concessions should be planned and should normally receive something in return.
Large or rapid concessions may create the impression that further movement is available.
The size and timing of concessions communicate information about priorities and limits.

sections.title: Avoid Unilateral Concessions
content: Do not give away valuable terms without considering what should be received in return.
Use conditional language such as: 'If you can provide the documentation by Friday, we can review the payment schedule.'
This connects movement by one party to movement by the other.

sections.title: Package Negotiation
content: Package negotiation combines several issues into one proposal.
This allows the parties to make trades across differently valued terms.
For example, price, delivery, warranty and training may be negotiated together.
A package reduces the risk that one issue is settled without understanding its relationship to the total agreement.
Multiple package proposals can also help reveal the other party's priorities.

sections.title: Example: Package Proposals
content: Option A offers a lower price with standard delivery and a 12-month warranty.
Option B offers a higher price with accelerated delivery and a 24-month warranty.
Option C offers the original price with standard delivery, operator training and additional spare parts.
The other party's response provides information about which terms it values most.

sections.title: Objective Criteria
content: Objective criteria are independent standards that can support fair and reasoned negotiation.
Examples include market prices, industry standards, technical calculations, historical data, contractual formulas and expert assessments.
Objective criteria reduce reliance on personal opinion or pressure.
The parties may still disagree about which criteria are appropriate.
However, a discussion based on evidence is generally more productive than unsupported demands.

sections.title: Emotions in Negotiation
content: Negotiations involve people and may produce frustration, fear, disappointment or anger.
Emotions can influence judgment, communication and willingness to cooperate.
A negotiator should recognize emotions without allowing them to control decisions.
Taking a break, restating facts or separating people from the problem may reduce tension.
Emotional reactions should not replace technical, commercial and contractual analysis.

sections.title: Do Not Personalize the Problem
content: Disagreement about a proposal is not necessarily a personal attack.
Focus on the issue, evidence and project consequences.
Personal criticism often reduces trust and makes agreement more difficult.

sections.title: Trust and Relationships
content: Many engineering projects involve long-term relationships between owners, contractors and suppliers.
Trust can improve information sharing, flexibility and problem-solving.
Trust does not mean avoiding contracts or accepting unsupported claims.
Professional trust combines openness with verification and clear documentation.
A negotiation outcome that damages an essential long-term relationship may create larger costs later.

sections.title: Reputation
content: Negotiators and organizations develop reputations based on how they behave.
A reputation for reliability and fairness may make future negotiations more efficient.
A reputation for breaking commitments, hiding information or using unreasonable pressure can reduce cooperation.
Short-term gains achieved through unfair behaviour may therefore create long-term commercial damage.

sections.title: Ethics in Negotiation
content: Ethical negotiation requires honesty regarding material facts, commitments and authority.
Negotiators should not knowingly make promises they cannot fulfil.
They should not falsify technical information, costs, approvals or contractual rights.
Ethical negotiation does not require disclosure of every internal priority or reservation point.
There is a difference between protecting confidential information and intentionally deceiving the other party.

sections.title: Misrepresentation
content: Misrepresenting technical performance, safety information, project authority or contractual facts can create serious legal and commercial consequences.
Negotiators should distinguish between persuasive presentation and false statements.

sections.title: Common Negotiation Tactics
content: Negotiators may use deadlines, high opening demands, limited authority, silence, repeated requests or package offers.
Some tactics are normal parts of negotiation, while others may be manipulative.
The best response is usually preparation, calm questioning and objective evaluation.
A negotiator should avoid reacting emotionally or making immediate concessions under pressure.
Understanding a tactic does not require responding with the same tactic.

sections.title: Example: Limited Authority
content: A supplier representative says: 'I would accept your proposal, but my director will never approve it.'
This may be a genuine authority limitation or a negotiation tactic.
The client can ask what terms the director could approve and whether the decision-maker should join the discussion.

sections.title: Deadlock
content: Deadlock occurs when the parties cannot make further progress.
It may result from incompatible limits, missing information, emotional conflict or excessive focus on one issue.
Possible responses include taking a break, changing negotiators, obtaining new information, introducing additional issues or using an independent facilitator.
The parties should also reconsider their alternatives.
Not every negotiation should end in agreement.

sections.title: Walking Away
content: A good negotiator must be prepared to reject an agreement that is worse than the available alternative.
Walking away should be based on analysis rather than anger.
The decision should consider technical, financial, contractual, operational and relational consequences.
A negotiated agreement is not automatically a good agreement.
Pressure to complete the negotiation should not override essential project requirements.

sections.title: Closing the Negotiation
content: A negotiation should close only when the parties understand the complete agreement.
The negotiator should summarize the agreed terms, unresolved matters, responsibilities and deadlines.
The parties should check that individual concessions form a consistent total package.
Ambiguous wording should be clarified before final acceptance.
The negotiators should also confirm whether additional internal approvals are required.

sections.title: Documenting the Agreement
content: Negotiated outcomes should be documented accurately and promptly.
Documentation may take the form of meeting minutes, a letter of agreement, a contract amendment, a purchase order or a formal change order.
The document should identify scope, price, schedule, responsibilities, assumptions and approval status.
Technical and commercial documents should be consistent.
A verbal agreement may be difficult to prove or implement when participants later remember it differently.

sections.title: Example: Incomplete Documentation
content: The parties agree that the supplier will accelerate delivery.
They do not document whether overtime cost is included, whether the warranty changes or what happens if the revised date is missed.
Although they agreed on the general objective, the missing details may create a later dispute.

sections.title: Implementation and Follow-Up
content: A negotiation is successful only if the agreement can be implemented.
The outcome should be communicated to the people responsible for execution.
Schedules, budgets, drawings, contracts and risk registers may need to be updated.
The parties should monitor whether agreed actions are completed.
Failure to translate the agreement into project controls can make the negotiation ineffective.

sections.title: Team Negotiation
content: Complex engineering negotiations are often conducted by teams.
The team may include a lead negotiator, technical specialist, commercial representative, contract manager and note-taker.
Roles should be agreed before the meeting.
The team should coordinate its messages and avoid contradicting itself in front of the other party.
Private internal discussions may be required before responding to important proposals.

sections.title: Example: Negotiation Team Roles
content: The project manager leads the discussion.
The engineering manager explains technical consequences.
The contract manager evaluates contractual rights.
The project controller verifies cost and schedule impacts.
A team member records proposals, concessions and action items.

sections.title: Internal Disagreement
content: Internal disagreement should normally be discussed privately rather than in front of the other party.
Conflicting messages can reveal weaknesses and create uncertainty about the organization's position.
The negotiation team should prepare and agree on its strategy before the meeting.

sections.title: Negotiating Technical Requirements
content: Technical requirements should be discussed using measurable criteria where possible.
The parties should distinguish mandatory requirements from preferences.
Changes should be assessed for their effects on performance, safety, compliance, cost and interfaces.
Technical concessions should not be made without understanding the complete project consequence.
The relevant engineers should participate when technical terms are negotiated.

sections.title: Example: Technical Compromise
content: A client requests a system capacity of 100 units per hour.
The supplier explains that achieving 100 requires a major increase in equipment size and cost.
Operational analysis shows that 90 units per hour is sufficient for nearly all expected conditions.
The parties may agree on 90 units per hour with an overload mode for exceptional situations.

sections.title: Negotiating Changes and Claims
content: Changes and claims often involve disagreement about scope, responsibility, entitlement, cost or time.
The parties should separate factual questions from commercial negotiation.
Relevant documentation may include contracts, drawings, instructions, schedules and cost records.
A negotiated settlement may be preferable to a lengthy dispute when it protects project progress and commercial relationships.
However, settlement decisions should be based on authority and a realistic assessment of rights and risks.

sections.title: Example: Change Negotiation
content: A contractor requests EUR 100,000 and ten additional days for a client-requested design change.
The client agrees that the change is outside the original scope but disputes the cost and schedule impact.
The parties review labour estimates, material cost, work sequencing and possible mitigation measures.
They agree on EUR 75,000 and five additional days, with the contractor adding a second work shift.

sections.title: Cultural Differences in Negotiation
content: International engineering projects involve different communication and negotiation traditions.
Cultures may differ in directness, hierarchy, decision speed, relationship building and attitudes toward disagreement.
A behaviour considered efficient in one culture may be considered disrespectful in another.
Negotiators should adapt their communication while maintaining clear objectives and professional standards.
Cultural awareness should inform preparation but should not lead to stereotypes about individuals.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: Effective negotiation begins before the parties enter the meeting room.
The strongest negotiators understand their alternatives, priorities, authority and evidence.
They listen carefully, ask disciplined questions and avoid making concessions without receiving value in return.
In engineering projects, a commercially attractive agreement is not successful if it creates unsafe, unclear or technically impossible obligations.
The best outcomes integrate technical feasibility, commercial value, contractual clarity and a workable relationship.

sections.title: Common Negotiation Mistakes
items: Beginning negotiations without clear objectives or authority limits.
Focusing only on stated positions instead of exploring underlying interests.
Negotiating only one issue, such as price, when several terms can be traded.
Making large or unconditional concessions too quickly.
Allowing time pressure to replace proper evaluation.
Failing to verify technical or commercial claims.
Revealing confidential limits or weak alternatives unnecessarily.
Treating disagreement as a personal conflict.
Allowing several team members to communicate inconsistent positions.
Accepting vague language in the final agreement.
Failing to document negotiated commitments.
Agreeing to terms that cannot be implemented operationally.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Negotiation is a process for reaching agreement where the parties have both shared and conflicting interests.
Negotiation occurs throughout the engineering project lifecycle.
Positions describe what parties request, while interests explain why those outcomes matter.
Distributive negotiation divides value, while integrative negotiation seeks to create additional value.
Preparation should define objectives, priorities, authority, alternatives and limits.
A reservation point identifies the least favourable acceptable agreement.
Strong alternatives improve negotiation power.
Questions and active listening help reveal interests and constraints.
Concessions should be deliberate, conditional and connected to reciprocal value.
Objective criteria improve fairness and decision quality.
Negotiated outcomes must be clearly documented and implemented.
A good engineering agreement must be technically feasible, commercially acceptable, contractually clear and operationally workable.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: What is the difference between a discussion and a negotiation?
Why is it useful to distinguish between a party's position and its underlying interests?
When is a distributive negotiation approach appropriate, and when may it be harmful?
How can several negotiable issues create opportunities for an integrative agreement?
Why should a negotiator define a reservation point before the meeting?
How can alternatives affect negotiation power?
Why should concessions normally be conditional?
What information should an engineering negotiation team prepare before discussing a proposed change?
How can objective criteria help resolve disagreement about price or schedule?
What risks arise when negotiated agreements are not documented clearly?
